questions.question: List the basic steps required to encrypt a message using the Playfair Cipher.
keyPoints: Prepare the plaintext by splitting it into digraphs and applying rules for double letters and odd length.
Generate the 5x5 key square using the keyword, removing duplicates and combining I/J.
For each digraph, apply the encryption rules based on their positions: same row (shift right), same column (shift down), or rectangle (swap columns).

questions.question: Construct the 5x5 Playfair matrix using the keyword 'MONARCHY' and encrypt the plaintext 'INSTRUMENTS'. Show all preparation and encryption steps.
keyPoints: Matrix construction: MONARCHY fills first, then remaining letters: M-O-N-A-R / C-H-Y-B-D / E-F-G-I-K / L-P-Q-S-T / U-V-W-X-Z.
Plaintext preparation: IN-ST-RU-ME-NT-SX (split into digraphs, pad with X if needed).
Apply rules for each digraph: e.g., IN forms a rectangle so swap columns, ST are in the same column so shift down.
Show the complete ciphertext result with each step justified by the applicable rule.

questions.question: Analyze the strengths and weaknesses of the Playfair Cipher compared to a simple monoalphabetic substitution cipher.
keyPoints: Strength: Encrypts digraphs, creating 676 possible substitutions vs. 26, making single-letter frequency analysis ineffective.
Strength: Practical for field use without special equipment, making it historically valuable for military communication.
Weakness: Still vulnerable to digraph frequency analysis with sufficient ciphertext.
Weakness: The filler letter X and predictable digraph patterns leak structural information.
Weakness: Completely broken by modern computational methods and known-plaintext attacks.

questions.question: Explain the three encryption rules of the Playfair cipher and state how each rule is reversed for decryption.
keyPoints: Same Row: Encryption shifts each letter RIGHT (wrapping). Decryption shifts each letter LEFT (wrapping).
Same Column: Encryption shifts each letter DOWN (wrapping). Decryption shifts each letter UP (wrapping).
Rectangle: Both encryption and decryption swap columns (each letter moves to the column of the other letter in the pair). The rectangle rule is self-inverse.
